I found several excellent recipes from RecipeTin Eats! Here are the most relevant results for your search:

## Top Result: Chicken Broccoli Stir Fry

A big batch stir fry made with chicken and broccoli, with lots and lots of sauce – because broccoli florets hold lots of sauce. The sauce uses the minimum ingredients but still makes something that stacks up to real Chinese take out.

Key features:
- Chinese cooking wine (shaoxing wine) is the restaurant secret – just a small splash adds depth of flavour into the sauce.
- Broccoli is steamed or boiled until cooked to your taste (it won't cook any further in the stir fry), usually microwaved for 3 minutes on high.
- Serve it over rice or, if you're pushing through a low carb phase, try cauliflower rice instead.

**Recipe link:** https://www.recipetineats.com/chicken-broccoli-stir-fry/

## Other Related Recipes Found:

- **Chicken Fried Rice** - https://www.recipetineats.com/chicken-fried-rice/
- **Chop Suey (Chicken Stir Fry)** - https://www.recipetineats.com/chicken-stir-fry-chop-suey/
- **Chicken Stir Fry with Rice Noodles** - https://www.recipetineats.com/chicken-stir-fry-with-rice-noodles/

These recipes all feature chicken, garlic, and can be served with rice, making them great options for your stir fry dinner!


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

I found several recipes from both Panlasang Pinoy and Kawaling Pinoy. Here are the relevant results:

**Chicken Fried Rice Recipes:**
Panlasang Pinoy's Chicken Fried Rice is seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil, with meaty flavor from chicken breast and minced onion and garlic.

Kawaling Pinoy's Chicken and Egg Fried Rice is a one-pan rice dish made with diced chicken breast, scrambled eggs broken into pieces, frozen green peas, garlic, green onions, and cold leftover rice.

**Garlic Fried Rice:**
Panlasang Pinoy's Garlic Fried Rice (Sinangag) is steamed rice fried with lots of garlic, a favorite side in the Philippines and most of Asia since rice is the staple food.

Kawaling Pinoy's Sinangag is the perfect use for day-old rice, loaded with fragrant garlic, making a delicious side dish.

Unfortunately, I didn't find a specific recipe that combines all three elements (chicken, broccoli, fried rice, and garlic) in a single recipe from these sites. However, you can find individual recipes for chicken fried rice and garlic fried rice that you could potentially combine, or look at their chicken with broccoli stir-fry recipes as an alternative.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Chicken with Broccoli Recipe - Key Details

**Hero/Main Image URL:**
`https://panlasangpinoy.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/02/Chicken-with-Broccoli.jpg`

**Prep Time:**
10 minutes

**Recipe Description:**
The page describes this as "an easy to prepare recipe that you can cook for your family during ordinary days. This dish is delicious." It's a straightforward chicken and broccoli dish prepared with oyster sauce, combining sliced chicken breast with broccoli florets in a savory sauce.

metric_results.reasoning: The response strongly follows expiry prioritisation: the critical items are chicken breast and broccoli (1 day), rice is near-term (2 days), and garlic is less urgent (4 days). The earliest-expiring ingredients appear immediately in the first meal’s expiringIngredients and usesIngredients, satisfying the requirement to feature the most urgent items prominently. Coverage is very high because all critical and near-term ingredients are represented across the meals’ expiringIngredients arrays, with only garlic not consistently marked there despite being used in multiple meals. Ordering is good since the first two meals both centre on chicken and broccoli, so only a minor deduction is warranted for not explicitly including the 4-day garlic in expiringIngredients more often.

results.ground_truth:
Should return a JSON array of 3-5 meal objects. At least one meal must include
"chicken breast" and "broccoli" in usesIngredients and expiringIngredients, since they expire
soonest. All objects must have mealName, suggestedMealType, prepTimeMinutes,
cuisine, description, usesIngredients, expiringIngredients, and missingIngredients.

agent_config.name: meal-recommender
agent_config.description: AI-powered meal recommendation agent that suggests meals to minimise food waste

metrics.name: ExpiryPrioritisation
metrics.criteria: Evaluate whether the meal suggestions prioritise ingredients with the earliest expiration dates, as reflected in the expiringIngredients and usesIngredients fields of each meal object.
evaluation_steps:
Parse the input to build an expiry-ordered list of ingredients: identify which items expire within 1-3 days (critical), within 4-7 days (near-term), and beyond 7 days or never (non-urgent). If no ingredient has an expiry date, score 1.0 immediately — the metric is not applicable.
For each meal object in actual_output, examine the expiringIngredients array. Check whether the ingredient(s) with the earliest expiry date appear in at least one meal's expiringIngredients. Heavily penalise (reduce score by at least 0.5) if the single most-urgent ingredient is absent from every meal's expiringIngredients and usesIngredients arrays.
Count what proportion of critical and near-term expiring ingredients from the input appear across all meals' expiringIngredients arrays. A higher proportion yields a higher score.
Evaluate ordering signal: meals that feature the most urgently expiring ingredients should appear earlier in the array. Reward responses where the first 1-2 meals centre on the soonest-expiring item; penalise responses where high-urgency meals appear only at the end.
Apply the following scoring rubric and return a score between 0.0 and 1.0: (0.0-0.2): No expiring ingredient from the input appears in any meal's
  expiringIngredients or usesIngredients — the agent has completely ignored expiry.
(0.3-0.5): Some expiring ingredients appear, but the single most-urgent item
  (earliest expiry date) is absent from every meal.
(0.6-0.7): The most-urgent ingredient is present in at least one meal, but
  coverage of other expiring items is poor (less than half represented), or
  high-urgency meals appear at the end of the array rather than the beginning.
(0.8-0.9): Most expiring ingredients are represented across the meal set and
  the most-urgent item is featured prominently; minor gaps in coverage or ordering.
(1.0): All or nearly all expiring ingredients are represented; the most urgently
  expiring ingredient appears in the first 1-2 meals; the array ordering clearly
  reflects expiry urgency.

test_cases.name: Prioritise expiring chicken

- 1 cup rice (expires: 2 days from now)

- 2 cloves garlic (expires: 4 days from now)
